title: Keeping smallholding livestock records
subtitle: The minimum legal records required for each livestock species and a simple system for keeping them up to date.
excerpt: UK livestock keepers are required by law to keep records of movements, births, deaths, and medicine use for most species. The records must be kept for a minimum of three years (cattle and pigs) or five to ten years (medicine records), be available for inspection by an APHA officer, and be up to date at all times. A one-page record per species covers all the legal requirements and takes under five minutes per week to maintain.
sourceNotes:
APHA, Livestock Keeper Guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/sheep-and-goat-keepers-responsibilities
Veterinary Medicines Directorate, medicine recording requirements. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/record-keeping-for-veterinary-medicines

content.text: on the day the first animal arrives. Do not wait for more animals, more space, or a better system. The entry date matters in law. Reconstructing it later from memory is unreliable and will not hold up at inspection.

Sheep and goats: each animal has an ear tag number (most are double-tagged). Record date of birth or arrival, the source holding CPH if you bought it, the date and destination CPH if you sold or moved it, and the date of death. Pigs: you do not have to tag pigs in the on-farm register. You do record batch arrivals (number of pigs, date of arrival, source CPH, eAML2 reference). The head count on the holding must always be right. Cattle: the individual passport is the main record. The on-farm register cross-references each animal's passport number.
Every medicine given to a food-producing animal must be recorded as a

. Keep a separate medicine record book for each species. For each entry record: the date of treatment, the animal or group treated (with identification), the product name and batch number, the dose, the route of administration, the withdrawal period, and the name of the person who administered it. Records must be kept for five years from the date of the entry.

A ring binder with one section per species works reliably and is easy to present at inspection. Use a printed template: movement in, movement out, death, and medicine entry columns. Update it the same day as the event, not at the weekend or at month-end. The most common inspection failure is records that are one to two weeks behind actual events, which cannot be reconstructed without relying on memory.

attrs.body: Some online movements (cattle in the Cattle Tracing System, sheep via EIDCymru or the APHA online system) must also be reported electronically within specified timeframes. The electronic report does not replace the on-farm register: you need both. Cattle movements must be reported to BCMS within three days; sheep movements in England must be entered into the keeper's holding record within 48 hours of the movement. These are separate obligations.
attrs.title: Online reporting alongside paper records
